name: ledger
description: The design ledger as checkable data: decisions carrying the alternatives they rejected, hypotheses carrying what would kill them, and debts carrying the condition that discharges them. A debt's params are ungrounded by construction, so `grounding/untrusted_via` can ask a gate — a release, a publication, a cut, a trade — whether anything unsound is about to pass through it. The point is not to write the caveat down; a README already does that, and a README cannot fire. The point is that the caveat goes red. Entries also declare what they `rests_on`, so a claim goes red when its grounds are superseded or deleted rather than waiting for someone to remember; `retraction` makes removal itself a recorded act, checkable against the roll committed beside the tree; and `supersedes`, on every entry kind and across them, is the one channel a correction travels by — the roll digests what each entry says, so a rewrite-in-place goes red instead of passing as an edit.

vocabulary.description: A choice made deliberately, kept so a later reader can tell it from an accident. Its children are the alternatives it rejected — as nodes, so 'what did we already rule out, and why' survives the rewrite that deletes the comment. A decision that is later reversed is not edited: the new decision `supersedes` it, and the core's supersession verb keeps both readable. A decision that only holds while some earlier one holds says so with `rests_on`, and stops being merely readable: it goes red when its grounds are withdrawn.

links.supersedes: the entry this one replaces. An entry that said something false is not edited (the roll digests what every entry says, and an unacknowledged rewrite goes red) and not retracted (it was valid; being wrong is not the same as never having belonged): the corrected entry supersedes it, both stay readable, and everything resting on the old one goes red until re-aimed. Cross-kind on purpose — a decision may moot a hypothesis, a corrected debt replaces the flawed one. Children (an alternative, a falsification, a discharge, a tombstone) do not declare it: a child has no standing of its own, and is corrected by superseding the entry it belongs to.
links.rests_on: the entries this one would not survive without — its grounds. Not decoration: `unsupported(self, 'rests_on')` counts the targets that have been superseded or deleted, and the rule below goes red while any of them has. This is the edge a flat ledger writes as prose ('the speed-up above is only sound because of it'), where nothing can act on it and a reader has to hold the whole list in mind to notice that the ground moved.

vocabulary.description: An option that was considered and NOT taken, and why not (payload.why). Its value is entirely in being findable later: most bad re-litigation is someone re-proposing a thing that was already rejected for a reason nobody wrote down.

A belief held provisionally — something believed but not yet established. It must carry at least one falsification child; a belief with no condition that would kill it is not a hypothesis, it is a mood. A hypothesis is also the kind most often invalidated sideways: not falsified by observation but rendered moot when the decision it was about is reversed, and there is nothing left to observe. That is what `rests_on` catches.

links.supersedes: the entry this one replaces. An entry that said something false is not edited (the roll digests what every entry says, and an unacknowledged rewrite goes red) and not retracted (it was valid; being wrong is not the same as never having belonged): the corrected entry supersedes it, both stay readable, and everything resting on the old one goes red until re-aimed. Cross-kind on purpose — a decision may moot a hypothesis, a corrected debt replaces the flawed one. Children (an alternative, a falsification, a discharge, a tombstone) do not declare it: a child has no standing of its own, and is corrected by superseding the entry it belongs to.
links.rests_on: the entries this one would not survive without — its grounds. Not decoration: `unsupported(self, 'rests_on')` counts the targets that have been superseded or deleted, and the rule below goes red while any of them has. This is the edge a flat ledger writes as prose ('the speed-up above is only sound because of it'), where nothing can act on it and a reader has to hold the whole list in mind to notice that the ground moved.

vocabulary.description: The observation that would kill the hypothesis above it. Carries a prose payload.claim and, where the claim is mechanically checkable, a payload.expr in the rule grammar plus payload.cadence — the same shape a watched criterion takes, so a ledger entry can graduate into a live check without being rewritten.

Something known-unsound, carried deliberately, with its cost stated rather than forgotten. Its params hold the unsound values and are NOT grounded — that is what makes the debt legible to `grounding/*` and therefore to a gate. Discharging a debt is not deleting the node: it is someone competent checking those params and grounding them, which is exactly the act the debt was waiting for. The substrate does not care who that is — only that it happened. A debt incurred BY a decision links `rests_on` at it: reverse the decision and the debt is no longer a price being paid, it is a leftover, and it goes red until someone says which. A debt whose PREMISE turns out false is neither paid nor invalid — it is superseded by the corrected debt, and stands.

links.supersedes: the entry this one replaces. An entry that said something false is not edited (the roll digests what every entry says, and an unacknowledged rewrite goes red) and not retracted (it was valid; being wrong is not the same as never having belonged): the corrected entry supersedes it, both stay readable, and everything resting on the old one goes red until re-aimed. Cross-kind on purpose — a decision may moot a hypothesis, a corrected debt replaces the flawed one. Children (an alternative, a falsification, a discharge, a tombstone) do not declare it: a child has no standing of its own, and is corrected by superseding the entry it belongs to.
links.rests_on: the entries this one would not survive without — its grounds. Not decoration: `unsupported(self, 'rests_on')` counts the targets that have been superseded or deleted, and the rule below goes red while any of them has. This is the edge a flat ledger writes as prose ('the speed-up above is only sound because of it'), where nothing can act on it and a reader has to hold the whole list in mind to notice that the ground moved.

vocabulary.description: The condition under which a debt is cleared — who must do what, and by when. A debt without one is not a debt, it is a permanent defect that has been dressed up as a plan. Its text is frozen like everything else: it says what must be done, not what was. The act of paying is grounding the debt's params, and the trace of payment — who checked, when — goes in the param's provenance `source`, which the roll deliberately does not digest. Recording a payment never requires rewriting a word.

A point past which unsound things must not travel: a release, a publication, a cut, a trade. It links `admits` at whatever it lets through, and the rule below counts the ungrounded params on the other end. A gate is the only place a ledger stops being a record and starts being a brake.

links.admits: the nodes this gate lets through — each must be sound

vocabulary.description: The only sanctioned way an entry leaves the ledger, and it is narrow on purpose: for something that was never a valid entry — a positioning statement filed as a hypothesis, a mood filed as a debt. NOT for an entry that was reversed or stated something false (that is `supersedes`, and the old entry stays), and NOT for a debt that was paid (that is `discharge`, and the debt stays grounded). Both of those keep the node, because the record of having been wrong is the part with the value in it. A retraction says only: this was never the kind of thing that belonged here. It carries a tombstone per entry buried, and those tombstones are what a roll check reads to tell a considered removal from a quiet one.

One buried entry: `payload.was` is the path it stood at, and `payload.why` is why it should never have stood there. The path matters more than it looks — a removal check compares the tree against the roll committed beside it, and matches what vanished against these. A tombstone with no `was` excuses nothing, which is the right way round: forgetting it leaves the check red, never green.

rules.name: a-decision-names-what-it-rejected
rules.description: A choice with no stated alternative is indistinguishable from an accident, and cannot be re-examined — there is nothing to re-examine it against.

rules.name: a-hypothesis-is-falsifiable
rules.description: A belief that cannot be killed by any observation is not being tested by the project; it is being assumed by it.

rules.name: a-debt-states-how-it-is-discharged
rules.description: An unsound thing carried with no discharge condition is not debt, it is decay. The condition is what lets anyone else finish it.

rules.name: nothing-unsound-passes-a-gate
rules.description: The load-bearing one. Whatever this gate admits must rest on observations, not on guesses: no ungrounded param may travel through it. This is the rule that turns a written-down caveat into a thing that stops a release.

rules.name: what-a-decision-rests-on-still-stands
rules.description: A decision taken because of an earlier one does not survive that one being reversed — it becomes a choice nobody would make again, still on the books, indistinguishable from a choice still held. This is the rule that makes the standing requirement mechanical: a decision whose stated rationale no longer holds should be discoverable by a rule, not by memory.

rules.name: what-a-hypothesis-rests-on-still-stands
rules.description: A belief can stop being worth holding without ever being tested: reverse the decision it was about and its falsification condition can no longer be observed at all. A hypothesis about a mechanism that no longer exists is not open, it is moot, and nothing in a flat ledger can tell them apart.

rules.name: what-a-debt-rests-on-still-stands
rules.description: A debt is the price of a decision. Reverse the decision and the price is either no longer owed or owed for a different reason — and either way the discharge condition beneath it was written against a world that is gone.

rules.name: a-retraction-names-what-it-buried
rules.description: A retraction with no tombstone is a deletion with a note attached, which is the thing it exists to replace. Each buried entry gets its own, so what left the ledger is as legible as what stayed in it.

examples.name: Validate every translation bundle against the reference bundle, key for key

payload.rationale: A bundle silently missing a key ships a blank string to a reader who cannot tell us, in a language we cannot read.
payload.consequence: Every copy change becomes an all-bundles change. That cost is real and was accepted with open eyes; it is recorded here so the next person meets it as a decision, not a surprise.

children.name: Fall back to the reference bundle for a missing key

payload.why: Cheaper per edit, but a visible fallback in the wrong language is a bug a reader reports; an invisible one is a bug nobody ever reports. Rejected — for now: if the edit cost bites, this is the first thing to revisit.

examples.name: Run the key-for-key check in CI, on every commit that touches copy

payload.rationale: Strict validation is only worth its cost if it is enforced where the cost is paid — at the edit — rather than discovered at release, when the edit is a week old and someone else's.

children.name: Run the check once, at release

payload.why: Same coverage, later signal. A missing key found at release blocks the release; found at the edit it is a typo. Rejected: the check is cheap enough that batching it buys nothing but distance.

examples.name: The generated bundles are good enough to publish unreviewed

payload.held_because: spot checks in the two languages we can read looked fine

children.name: A reader of that language reports the copy as wrong or unidiomatic

payload.claim: One credible report of broken copy in any generated bundle falsifies this and the bundles must be read.

examples.name: A bundle that WAS generated unchecked, and has since been read

strings.source: checked by a reader of that language, 2026-07

children.name: Someone who reads the language checks every string

examples.name: Strings shipped unread, counted from the generator's log

payload.premise: every generated bundle appears in the log, so the log's count is the whole exposure

children.name: Count the unread strings in the generator log

payload.who: anyone who can read the log

examples.name: Strings shipped unread, counted at publication — the log held failures only

payload.correction: the log recorded generation failures, so counting it counted the wrong thing; the count now happens where bundles actually ship

children.name: Someone who reads the language checks every shipped string

examples.name: Publication: nothing unsound reaches a reader
Filed as ledger entries, but not the kind of thing a ledger holds

payload.why: Both were opinions about how the work should look, wearing the grammar of a claim. Neither could be superseded (nothing replaced them) or discharged (nothing was owed), so leaving them in would have meant carrying two entries no rule could ever act on — and quietly deleting them would have left the removal check red with no way to say why.

children.name: 'The copy should read well' — filed as a hypothesis

payload.why: A preference, not a belief: no observation could have killed it, and the falsification child written under it restated the preference in the future tense.

nodes.name: We went with the obvious thing

payload.rationale: it seemed right at the time

counter_examples.because: a choice with no rejected alternative

nodes.name: We'll clean this up at some point

counter_examples.because: an unsound thing carried with no way to finish it

nodes.name: A machine-generated bundle nobody has read

children.name: Someone who reads the language checks every string

counter_examples.because: a release that publishes a value nobody ever checked

nodes.name: Parse each bundle once and cache it
Parse on every request after all — the cache went stale in prod

children.name: Keep the cache and invalidate harder

nodes.name: Invalidate the bundle cache whenever copy is written

payload.why: wrong copy for the length of the TTL

counter_examples.because: a decision still on the books whose whole reason was reversed

nodes.name: Invalidate the bundle cache whenever copy is written

payload.why: wrong copy for the length of the TTL

counter_examples.because: a decision resting on an entry that was deleted out from under it

nodes.name: Parse each bundle once and cache it
Parse on every request after all

children.name: Keep the cache and invalidate harder

nodes.name: The bundle cache is never read after copy changed

children.name: A reader is served a string that was edited

counter_examples.because: a belief about a mechanism that no longer exists — moot, not open

nodes.name: Parse each bundle once and cache it
Parse on every request after all

children.name: Keep the cache and invalidate harder

nodes.name: The cache is sized by a number nobody measured

children.name: Measure the working set under real traffic

counter_examples.because: a price still recorded as owed for a decision nobody took in the end

nodes.name: Removed some entries that were not pulling their weight

counter_examples.because: a deletion with a note attached, which is what a retraction replaces
